heroOverview: Mediastinitis is a severe infection of the mediastinum — the central chest cavity housing the heart, major vessels, trachea, and esophagus — carrying an untreated mortality that exceeds 50%. It arises most commonly after cardiac surgery or from a perforated esophagus, and less often by spread of a deep neck infection or dental abscess tracking downward along fascial planes. Roughly 0.3–3% of patients undergoing median sternotomy develop post-surgical mediastinitis, making it the dominant cause in contemporary practice. The infection triggers intense local destruction, systemic sepsis, and can erode the pericardium or great vessels within days. Survival depends on early recognition, aggressive surgical debridement, and prolonged intravenous antibiotics, with modern combined protocols achieving survival rates of 70–90% in post-operative cases.

keyStats.prevalence: Occurs in 0.3–3% of median sternotomy patients; esophageal perforation-related mediastinitis is rarer, affecting roughly 0.05 per 100,000 people annually
keyStats.demographics: Adults who have undergone open-heart surgery (median age ~65 years) represent the largest group; all ages at risk from descending odontogenic infections
keyStats.avgAge: Most post-surgical cases occur in patients aged 55–75 who have undergone coronary bypass or valve surgery
keyStats.globalCases: An estimated 8,000–15,000 post-sternotomy mediastinitis cases occur annually worldwide

definition: Mediastinitis (ICD-10: J98.51) is polymicrobial or monomicrobial infection of the loose connective tissue and structures of the mediastinum, the space within the chest bounded by the sternum anteriorly, the thoracic vertebrae posteriorly, the lungs laterally, the thoracic inlet superiorly, and the diaphragm inferiorly. Infection spreads rapidly through this compartment because the tissue planes offer little resistance. Two mechanistic categories dominate: post-operative mediastinitis, which follows median sternotomy and results from wound contamination during or after surgery; and descending necrotising mediastinitis, where oropharyngeal or cervical infection, often from a dental abscess, Ludwig angina, or esophageal perforation, tracks inferiorly via deep cervical fascial planes. Organisms in post-surgical cases are typically Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), coagulase-negative staphylococci, and Gram-negative rods; descending infections are usually polymicrobial with oral streptococci and anaerobes. Fibrosing mediastinitis, a non-infectious granulomatous variant caused by Histoplasma capsulatum or sarcoid, is a separate entity with a chronic, obstructive course. Classification systems — notably El Oakley and Wright for post-operative cases — grade severity and guide surgical decision-making.

typesClassification.description: The most common form in high-income countries, occurring within 30 days of median sternotomy. Caused by wound contamination with skin flora, mainly Staphylococcus aureus and coagulase-negative staphylococci, it presents with sternal wound dehiscence, drainage, and systemic sepsis.

Rapidly spreading polymicrobial infection that descends from the oropharynx or neck — often from a dental abscess, Ludwig angina, peritonsillar abscess, or esophageal perforation — tracking along fascial planes into the mediastinum. Carries the highest mortality, often exceeding 30–40% even with surgery.

Follows rupture of the esophagus from instrumentation, foreign body, Boerhaave syndrome, or malignancy. Gastric contents and oral bacteria flood the mediastinum, causing chemical and bacterial destruction.

A non-infectious, granulomatous process driven by an excessive immunological response to Histoplasma capsulatum or sarcoidosis. Characterized by dense fibrous tissue that compresses mediastinal structures over months to years rather than by acute infection.

primarySymptoms: Fever above 38°C with rigors and diaphoresis, reflecting systemic infection spreading through the mediastinal space.
Severe central chest pain, often pleuritic (worse with breathing or swallowing), caused by inflammation of the pericardium and mediastinal pleura.
Sternal wound instability or dehiscence — clicking, separation, or purulent drainage from the incision site in post-operative patients.
Dysphagia or odynophagia (painful swallowing), prominent when the infection involves or originates from the esophagus.
Neck swelling, redness, or crepitus extending from the jaw or throat toward the chest in descending cervical infections.
Dyspnoea and tachypnoea resulting from encroachment on the trachea, main bronchi, or pleural space.
Subcutaneous emphysema — a crackling sensation under the skin of the neck and chest caused by air tracking from a perforation.
Rapidly progressing septic shock: hypotension, tachycardia, mottled skin, and oliguria indicating overwhelming systemic infection.
Hoarseness or stridor when infection compresses the trachea or recurrent laryngeal nerve.
Back pain radiating to the interscapular region, particularly with posterior mediastinal involvement or descending infections.
earlyWarningSigns: Low-grade fever and wound discomfort in the days following median sternotomy — do not dismiss as routine post-operative inflammation.
A subtle clicking sensation over the sternum when coughing, suggesting early sternal instability before overt dehiscence.
Increasing neck swelling or trismus (difficulty opening the jaw) in a patient with a recent dental procedure or tooth abscess.
Unexplained tachycardia or rising inflammatory markers (CRP, white cell count) several days after cardiac surgery.
emergencySigns: Sternal wound openly separating with visible pus or mediastinal tissue, requiring immediate surgical consultation.
Rapid-onset dyspnoea with stridor suggesting tracheal compression — a surgical airway emergency.
Signs of septic shock: systolic blood pressure below 90 mmHg, heart rate above 120 bpm, reduced consciousness, and oliguria.
Subcutaneous emphysema of the neck and chest following vomiting or esophageal instrumentation, consistent with Boerhaave syndrome.
Cardiac tamponade — muffled heart sounds, hypotension, and distended neck veins — indicating pericardial involvement.

causes.description: Intra-operative skin flora — principally Staphylococcus aureus, MRSA, and coagulase-negative staphylococci — contaminate the sternal wound during or immediately after cardiac surgery. The avascular bone, retained bone wax, and impaired tissue perfusion in patients with diabetes or obesity create an environment where even small inocula can establish deep infection within days.

Sudden pressure-related rupture of the esophagus or laceration during upper endoscopy, dilation, or biopsy releases salivary and gastric contents directly into the mediastinum. The acid, pepsin, bile, and mixed oral-gastric bacteria produce a combined chemical and polymicrobial infection that progresses rapidly within 12–24 hours.

Deep neck infections from dental abscesses, Ludwig angina, peritonsillar abscesses, or vertebral osteomyelitis track inferiorly along the retrovisceral, prevertebral, or vascular fascial compartments, reaching the posterior mediastinum. The route is anatomically direct: the retropharyngeal space communicates freely with the posterior mediastinum through the thoracic inlet.

causes.cause: Haematogenous or direct spread from adjacent infection
causes.description: Bacteraemia from any source — endocarditis, pneumonia, vertebral osteomyelitis — can seed mediastinal tissue. Less commonly, a subdiaphragmatic abscess or transdiaphragmatic spread from a hepatic or subphrenic collection reaches the inferior mediastinum.

causes.cause: Foreign body or penetrating chest trauma
causes.description: Ingested foreign bodies that perforate the esophagus, or penetrating wounds that violate the mediastinum, introduce bacteria directly into the space. In children, coin or fish-bone ingestion is a recognised but uncommon cause.

riskFactors.description: Any patient undergoing open-heart surgery through a median sternotomy faces a baseline infection risk of 0.3–3%; bilateral internal mammary artery harvesting, prolonged cardiopulmonary bypass, and re-operation further increase risk.

Patients with diabetes have approximately three-fold higher rates of post-sternotomy mediastinitis than non-diabetics, attributed to impaired neutrophil function, hyperglycaemia favouring microbial growth, and reduced tissue perfusion; perioperative glycaemic control (targeting blood glucose 140–180 mg/dL) meaningfully reduces incidence.

Excess subcutaneous and mediastinal fat impairs wound perfusion, increases dead-space, and raises wound tension, all of which promote infection after sternotomy; the relative risk is roughly double compared with normal-weight patients.

Pre-existing esophageal strictures, malignancy, or prior radiation weaken the esophageal wall, and upper gastrointestinal endoscopy and dilation procedures carry a small but real perforation risk, the primary cause of non-surgical mediastinitis.

Untreated tooth abscesses, severe periodontitis, peritonsillar abscesses, and Ludwig angina are the principal source conditions for descending necrotising mediastinitis; prompt treatment of dental disease before elective cardiac surgery reduces this pathway.

Solid organ transplant recipients, patients on long-term steroids or chemotherapy, and people with HIV-related immunodeficiency mount an attenuated response that allows infections to spread further before presenting clinically.

Each additional hour of surgical time and every previous sternotomy increases contamination exposure and disrupts tissue architecture and blood supply, raising infection risk by an estimated 1.5-fold per re-operation.

affectedDemographics: Adults aged 55–75 undergoing coronary artery bypass grafting or valve replacement surgery
Patients with poorly controlled diabetes mellitus and obesity, particularly those requiring cardiac surgery
People with untreated or severe dental disease or Ludwig angina, at risk for descending cervical spread
Individuals with esophageal disorders — strictures, malignancy, or achalasia — who undergo dilation or endoscopy
Immunocompromised individuals, including transplant recipients and those receiving long-term corticosteroids
Children and young adults who ingest sharp foreign bodies that perforate the esophagus
diagnosisOverview: Mediastinitis is a clinical and radiological diagnosis in which delay is dangerous. In post-operative patients, the hallmarks are fever, sternal wound instability, tenderness, and drainage within 30 days of sternotomy. Blood cultures and wound swabs are drawn but do not delay surgical exploration when clinical suspicion is high. Computed tomography (CT) of the chest with contrast is the imaging gold standard: it shows mediastinal widening, air or fluid collections, sternal dehiscence, and involvement of adjacent structures, and it guides the extent of debridement and defines the drainage route. Plain chest radiography is inadequate and may appear misleadingly normal in the early stages. Laboratory findings consistently show a markedly elevated white cell count, C-reactive protein, procalcitonin, and lactate in septic cases. In suspected descending necrotising mediastinitis, CT of the neck and chest together is essential to map the full extent of fascial involvement before surgery. Esophageal perforation is confirmed by a contrast swallow using water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) followed by dilute barium if the first study is negative; CT oesophagography has largely replaced conventional fluoroscopy in centres with 24-hour CT access. Microbiological cultures from blood, wound, and intra-operative specimens direct the antibiotic regimen, with special attention to MRSA and Gram-negative resistance patterns.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines the extent of mediastinal involvement, identifies air or fluid collections, detects sternal dehiscence, and maps fascial spread into the neck or pleural cavity.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You lie still while the scanner rotates around you; an iodine-based dye is injected into a vein in your arm to highlight infected tissue. The scan itself takes 3–5 minutes and results are typically read within the hour in acute settings.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities to guide targeted therapy, essential for detecting bacteraemia and MRSA.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Blood is drawn from two separate venepuncture sites before starting antibiotics; cultures are incubated and reported over 24–72 hours, though antibiotic cover begins empirically immediately.

diagnosticTests.test: Wound swab and deep tissue culture
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides the most reliable microbiological identification from the infection site, distinguishing colonisation from invasive pathogen in post-operative wounds.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab or tissue sample is taken by a clinician from the wound or during surgical exploration; results take 24–48 hours.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count, CRP, procalcitonin, and lactate
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies the systemic inflammatory response, supports the diagnosis of sepsis, and monitors treatment response; procalcitonin above 2 ng/mL is highly suggestive of bacterial infection.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard venous blood draw; results are available within 1–2 hours in hospital laboratories.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms esophageal perforation and localises the leak when this is the suspected source, essential before surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: You swallow a small cup of water-soluble contrast liquid while standing in front of an X-ray screen; the radiologist watches the dye travel down the esophagus and identifies any leakage. The test takes 10–20 minutes.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses for pericardial effusion, cardiac tamponade, or endocarditis in patients with post-cardiac-surgery mediastinitis, and guides pericardiocentesis if needed.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A probe is placed on the chest wall (transthoracic) or, for better images, into the esophagus under sedation (transoesophageal); the test is painless for the transthoracic approach and takes 20–30 minutes.

treatmentOverview: Mediastinitis is a surgical emergency: antibiotics alone do not save most patients. The cornerstone of treatment is urgent operative debridement — removing all infected, necrotic bone and tissue, irrigating the mediastinum, and establishing adequate drainage. Post-sternotomy cases are re-opened via the same incision; descending infections require cervicotomy and, depending on extent, subxiphoid or thoracotomy drainage. Two surgical strategies dominate: open wound management with repeated debridement and delayed closure, which was the historical standard; and vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) with sternal rewiring, which has shortened intensive-care stays and improved survival to 70–90% in recent series. VAC devices apply negative pressure to the wound, continuously remove exudate, reduce bacterial load, and promote granulation tissue, enabling earlier closure and chest wall stabilisation. Empirical antibiotic therapy — typically vancomycin for MRSA coverage plus a broad-spectrum beta-lactam or carbapenem for Gram-negative organisms — is started immediately and then narrowed based on culture results. Duration is usually 4–6 weeks of intravenous therapy for confirmed mediastinitis, sometimes extended for bony sternal osteomyelitis. Esophageal perforation cases require control of the leak (primary repair, exclusion, stenting, or resection depending on timing and extent), followed by mediastinal irrigation and drainage. Nutritional support — enteral or parenteral — is critical because these patients are profoundly catabolic. ICU care with cardiorespiratory monitoring, vasopressor support if in shock, and meticulous glycaemic control are standard throughout.

medicalTreatments.name: Vancomycin
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical first-line intravenous antibiotic for MRSA and coagulase-negative staphylococci, the dominant organisms in post-sternotomy infection; dose is adjusted to therapeutic trough levels and continued until cultures guide de-escalation.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Achieves bacteriological clearance in approximately 70–85% of susceptible staphylococcal mediastinitis cases when combined with surgery; resistance (vancomycin-resistant enterococci) is uncommon but rising.

medicalTreatments.name: Broad-spectrum beta-lactam or carbapenem (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam, meropenem)
medicalTreatments.description: Added to vancomycin for empirical coverage of Gram-negative rods and anaerobes, particularly in descending cervical infections and esophageal perforations where polymicrobial flora predominates.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combination regimens achieve infection control in conjunction with surgery in over 80% of cases; monotherapy is inadequate for the polymicrobial burden of descending infections.

medicalTreatments.name: Daptomycin
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative intravenous lipopeptide for MRSA and vancomycin-intermediate staphylococci when vancomycin minimum inhibitory concentrations are elevated; lung activity is limited, so it is combined with other agents for mixed infection.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Non-inferior to vancomycin for Gram-positive bacteraemia in clinical trials; used in approximately 15–20% of complex mediastinitis cases at specialist centres.

medicalTreatments.name: Antifungal therapy (fluconazole or echinocandin)
medicalTreatments.description: Added when Candida is isolated from wound cultures or blood, which occurs in roughly 5–10% of post-sternotomy infections, particularly in immunocompromised patients or those on prolonged broad-spectrum antibiotics.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Echinocandins achieve clearance of candidaemia in approximately 70–80% of cases; untreated fungal mediastinitis is almost universally fatal.

medicalTreatments.name: Fibrosing mediastinitis: antifungal therapy (itraconazole) and corticosteroids
medicalTreatments.description: For Histoplasma-related fibrosing mediastinitis, itraconazole may slow progression in active fungal disease; corticosteroids are used selectively for inflammatory obstruction but their benefit is uncertain and they are not used for acute bacterial mediastinitis.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Fibrosing mediastinitis responds poorly to medical therapy; only 25–30% of patients show meaningful symptom improvement with antifungals alone.

surgicalOptions.name: Surgical debridement with vacuum-assisted closure (VAC)
surgicalOptions.description: Re-sternotomy to remove infected tissue and necrotic bone, followed by placement of a foam VAC dressing and closure of the skin, with the negative-pressure device changed every 2–3 days until the wound is clean enough for definitive closure. Now the preferred approach at most cardiac surgical centres.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival rates of 70–90% in post-sternotomy mediastinitis when combined with appropriate antibiotics, compared with 50–60% for open packing alone in older series.

surgicalOptions.name: Open packing with delayed closure
surgicalOptions.description: The wound is left open and packed with antiseptic-soaked dressings, changed daily in the ICU under sedation, until infection is controlled and granulation tissue appears (typically 5–14 days), before definitive closure. Historical standard now largely replaced by VAC but still used when VAC is not available.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Survival approximately 60–75% in post-sternotomy cases; higher morbidity than VAC due to prolonged open chest exposure.

surgicalOptions.name: Muscle flap reconstruction (pectoralis major, omental)
surgicalOptions.description: After debridement, well-vascularised muscle — usually the pectoralis major or, for inferior defects, the omentum — is transposed to obliterate dead space and bring blood supply to the infected field, often in combination with VAC therapy for complex or large defects.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Reduces recurrent infection and facilitates primary healing in 80–90% of patients with large sternal defects.

surgicalOptions.name: Cervicotomy and mediastinal drainage for descending necrotising mediastinitis
surgicalOptions.description: Neck incision to drain the primary cervical focus, combined with subxiphoid, transthoracic, or video-assisted thoracoscopic drainage of mediastinal collections, tailored to the anatomical extent mapped by pre-operative CT.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Mortality remains 25–40% even with surgery due to the aggressive polymicrobial nature; early surgery (within 24 hours of CT diagnosis) achieves the best outcomes.

alternativeTreatments.name: Hyperbaric oxygen therapy (adjunctive)
alternativeTreatments.description: Used at specialist centres as an adjunct to surgery and antibiotics in refractory or chronic mediastinitis; increased tissue oxygen tension may improve leukocyte bactericidal activity and impair anaerobic growth. Evidence is limited to case series.

alternativeTreatments.name: Continuous mediastinal irrigation
alternativeTreatments.description: Insertion of irrigation and drain tubes to flush the mediastinal space with antibiotic or antiseptic solution, used as an adjunct to debridement in some centres; evidence is weaker than for VAC but it remains in use, particularly in descending infections.

whySeeSpecialist: Mediastinitis demands immediate involvement of a cardiothoracic surgeon, an infectious disease specialist, and an intensivist. Delay of even hours in surgical debridement worsens mortality. Any patient with fever plus sternal wound problems after cardiac surgery, neck swelling with a dental history, or suspected esophageal perforation must be assessed urgently by a surgeon at a centre with 24-hour CT, cardiac surgery, and intensive care capabilities. Outcomes at high-volume centres with dedicated cardiac surgical infection programs are meaningfully better than at facilities without this expertise.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek a cardiothoracic surgeon with specific experience in sternal reconstruction and vacuum-assisted closure, supported by an infectious disease physician familiar with MRSA and polymicrobial infections. For descending cervical cases, an ear-nose-throat or head-and-neck surgeon may be needed for the cervicotomy component. Choose a hospital with 24-hour CT, cardiac ICU, and expertise in VAC management. Ask about annual case volumes for post-sternotomy mediastinitis, as higher-volume centres consistently achieve better survival.
preventionStrategies: Optimise blood glucose perioperatively; target 140–180 mg/dL during and after cardiac surgery to reduce staphylococcal wound infection by approximately 66%.
Administer prophylactic cefazolin within 60 minutes before sternotomy incision and continue for no more than 24–48 hours post-operatively per major guidelines.
Minimise use of bone wax, which impairs bone healing and increases infection risk; use BioGlue or haemostatic cellulose agents as alternatives.
Treat dental infections and abscesses before elective cardiac surgery to eliminate the odontogenic source for descending mediastinitis.
Use bilateral internal mammary artery grafting judiciously in diabetic patients given the associated 2–3-fold increase in sternal infection risk.
Apply nasal decolonisation (intranasal mupirocin for 5 days pre-operatively) for patients with confirmed S. aureus nasal carriage to reduce post-sternotomy MRSA infection.
lifestyleModifications: Maintain optimal glycaemic control before and after surgery; if you have diabetes, work with your endocrinologist to achieve an HbA1c below 8% ahead of planned cardiac procedures.
Achieve a healthy weight before elective cardiac surgery; a BMI reduction from obese range to overweight category measurably lowers post-sternotomy wound infection rates.
Attend all dental check-ups and treat active infections before any planned major chest surgery.
Avoid smoking, which impairs wound healing and tissue oxygenation; even a 4-week cessation period before surgery reduces pulmonary and wound complications.
Follow all wound care instructions after sternotomy: keep the incision dry, avoid lifting more than 2–3 kg for 6 weeks, and report any redness, discharge, or clicking to your surgeon promptly.

recommended: High-protein diet (1.5–2 g/kg/day) to support wound healing and immune function during recovery
Adequate calorie intake to prevent the significant muscle wasting associated with prolonged critical illness
Vitamin C and zinc supplementation, which support collagen synthesis and wound healing
Enteral nutrition (via nasogastric tube if unable to eat) in the ICU phase, preferred over parenteral nutrition to preserve gut barrier function
avoid: High-sugar foods and concentrated carbohydrates that destabilise blood glucose, particularly important in diabetic patients
Alcohol during active infection and antibiotic therapy
Delayed feeding: prolonged fasting worsens catabolism and impairs immunity

exerciseGuidelines: Activity during the acute phase of mediastinitis is essentially impossible, as most patients are critically ill in intensive care. During the recovery phase after wound closure, a carefully supervised cardiac rehabilitation programme is essential. Sternal precautions — no pushing, pulling, or lifting above 2–3 kg — apply for at least 6–8 weeks until sternal stability is confirmed by the surgical team. Gradual ambulation under physiotherapy guidance begins as soon as haemodynamic stability allows, as immobility increases the risk of venous thromboembolism and pneumonia.
dailyManagement: Complete the full intravenous antibiotic course, typically 4–6 weeks; do not stop early even if you feel well — premature cessation risks relapse.
Attend all wound check appointments; report any redness, warmth, discharge, or clicking sensation at the sternum immediately.
Monitor blood glucose daily if you have diabetes; tight control significantly improves wound healing.
Follow sternal precautions strictly during the first 6–8 weeks post-surgery to allow the sternum to heal.
Maintain adequate nutrition and protein intake throughout recovery; consider high-protein supplements if appetite is poor.
prognosis: The prognosis for mediastinitis has improved substantially with vacuum-assisted closure and early aggressive surgery, but it remains a serious condition with meaningful mortality. Post-sternotomy mediastinitis managed with modern VAC protocols achieves survival rates of 70–90% in major series, though long-term quality of life may be affected by prolonged hospitalisation and sternal instability. Descending necrotising mediastinitis carries a hospital mortality of 25–40% even with surgery, rising to over 70% without operative intervention. Survivors often require prolonged intensive care, multiple surgical procedures, and months of rehabilitation. Esophageal perforation-related mediastinitis mortality correlates strongly with time to treatment: perforation treated within 24 hours carries a mortality of 10–25%, while delayed diagnosis beyond 24 hours raises mortality to 40–60%. Fibrosing mediastinitis is a chronic, slowly progressive disease; the prognosis depends on which structures are compressed — superior vena cava obstruction and pulmonary artery narrowing are the most life-threatening complications.
recoveryTimeline: Most patients with post-sternotomy mediastinitis spend 2–4 weeks in intensive care and a further 4–8 weeks in hospital for wound management and intravenous antibiotics. Discharge to home or a rehabilitation facility typically occurs at 6–10 weeks from the re-operation, provided the wound is closed and the patient is on oral antibiotics. Full sternal healing takes 3–6 months, during which physical restrictions apply. Return to normal activity may take 6–12 months. Descending and esophageal cases require comparable or longer timelines, depending on the number of surgical procedures required.
complications: Sternal osteomyelitis and non-union — persistent bone infection or failure of the sternum to heal, requiring repeated debridement or permanent prosthetic chest wall reconstruction.
Septic shock and multi-organ failure — the principal cause of death; kidneys, liver, and lungs are most vulnerable to hypoperfusion during mediastinal sepsis.
Cardiac tamponade — accumulation of infected pericardial fluid compressing the heart, requiring emergent pericardiocentesis or surgical drainage.
Mediastinal haemorrhage — erosion of a major vessel by infection, particularly a risk when the aortic suture line or bypass grafts are involved.
Chronic draining sinus — persistent wound tract indicating residual infected bone or foreign material (pledgets, bone wax) that failed to clear.
Osteomyelitis of adjacent ribs or vertebrae — extension beyond the sternum requiring prolonged antibiotic therapy and potentially further debridement.

supportResources.name: Society of Thoracic Surgeons — Patient Resources
supportResources.description: Patient information on cardiac surgery outcomes, including wound complication management, from the major cardiothoracic surgery society.

supportResources.name: CDC — Surgical Site Infection Prevention
supportResources.description: U.S. CDC guidance on preventing surgical site infections, including perioperative antibiotic protocols relevant to sternotomy patients.

supportResources.name: NHS — Recovering from Heart Surgery
supportResources.description: UK patient guidance on the post-cardiac surgery recovery period, including warning signs for wound complications.

sources.title: El Oakley RM, Wright JE. Postoperative mediastinitis: classification and management. Ann Thorac Surg. 1996
Robicsek F, Fokin A. Poststernotomy mediastinitis and vacuum-assisted wound closure. Ann Thorac Surg. 2000
Pastene B et al. Descending necrotising mediastinitis: diagnosis, management and outcomes. Intensive Care Med. 2015
Fowler VG et al. Staphylococcus aureus endocarditis and mediastinitis: epidemiology and outcomes. JAMA. 2005
Society of Thoracic Surgeons — Perioperative Antibiotic Prophylaxis Guidelines
NICE — Surgical Site Infections: Prevention and Treatment (NG125)
Gualis J et al. Risk factors for mediastinitis and endocarditis after cardiac surgery. Asian Cardiovasc Thorac Ann. 2009

confusedWithConditions.name: Pneumonia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Pneumonia causes fever and chest pain with alveolar consolidation on imaging; mediastinitis shows mediastinal widening, air collections, and often a sternal wound problem rather than a parenchymal lung infiltrate.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cardiac tamponade
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tamponade produces the Beck triad of hypotension, muffled heart sounds, and distended neck veins without the fever and wound signs characteristic of mediastinitis; the two can coexist when pericarditis complicates mediastinitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thoracic aortic dissection
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Aortic dissection causes sudden tearing chest pain radiating to the back with a mediastinal shadow on X-ray but no fever, leukocytosis, or wound findings; CT with contrast clearly distinguishes the two.

confusedWithConditions.name: Ludwig angina
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Ludwig angina is a submandibular space infection that can descend into the mediastinum; early Ludwig angina is confined to the floor of the mouth and neck, but once spread to the chest it becomes a component of descending mediastinitis.

relatedConditions.name: Ludwig angina
relatedConditions.relevance: Ludwig angina is the most common single source condition for descending necrotising mediastinitis, spreading from the submandibular space through cervical fascial planes into the mediastinum.

relatedConditions.name: Dental abscess
relatedConditions.relevance: Odontogenic abscesses that spread deeply rather than pointing superficially are the upstream cause of most descending mediastinitis cases, emphasising the importance of early dental treatment.

relatedConditions.name: Pleural effusion
relatedConditions.relevance: Mediastinal infection frequently extends to involve the pleural space; a parapneumonic pleural effusion or empyema on CT should prompt consideration of mediastinitis as the underlying source.

relatedConditions.name: Odontogenic infection
relatedConditions.relevance: Periodontal and peri-apical infections that breach the jaw cortex and enter the deep neck spaces are the dominant microbial pathway for descending necrotising mediastinitis.

faqs.question: What is mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Mediastinitis is a serious infection of the mediastinum — the central chest compartment containing the heart, major blood vessels, trachea, and esophagus. It most often follows open-heart surgery or a tear in the esophagus, and spreads rapidly if not treated surgically.

faqs.question: How common is mediastinitis after heart surgery?
faqs.answer: Post-sternotomy mediastinitis occurs in roughly 0.3 to 3% of patients who undergo open-heart surgery through a median sternotomy. That translates to thousands of cases per year worldwide, making it the most frequent cause of mediastinitis in high-income countries.

faqs.question: What are the warning signs of mediastinitis after cardiac surgery?
faqs.answer: Key warning signs are fever, a sternal wound that feels unstable or clicks when you cough, wound redness or drainage, and feeling generally unwell in the days to weeks after surgery. Any of these should prompt immediate contact with your surgical team.

faqs.question: Can a tooth infection cause mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. A severe dental abscess or Ludwig angina can spread along fascial planes from the floor of the mouth and neck down into the mediastinum, a process called descending necrotising mediastinitis. This is why untreated dental infections should be taken seriously, especially before planned surgery.

faqs.question: How is mediastinitis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment requires urgent surgery to remove infected tissue (debridement) combined with prolonged intravenous antibiotics, usually vancomycin and a broad-spectrum agent for 4–6 weeks. Vacuum-assisted wound closure devices are now the standard after re-sternotomy and have significantly improved survival rates.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: With modern vacuum-assisted closure and early surgical debridement, post-sternotomy mediastinitis has a survival rate of 70–90% at specialist centres. Descending necrotising mediastinitis from dental or neck infections carries a higher mortality of 25–40% even with surgery, reflecting its aggressive polymicrobial nature.

faqs.question: How long does recovery from mediastinitis take?
faqs.answer: Most patients spend 2–4 weeks in intensive care followed by another 4–8 weeks in hospital for wound management and antibiotics. Returning home typically takes 6–10 weeks from re-operation, and full sternal healing may take 3–6 months. Full activity recovery can take 6–12 months.

faqs.question: Is mediastinitis life-threatening?
faqs.answer: Yes. Mediastinitis is a medical emergency with mortality exceeding 50% if not treated promptly with surgery and antibiotics. The infection spreads rapidly through the chest, can cause septic shock, and can erode major blood vessels or compress the heart. It should always be treated in a hospital with cardiothoracic surgical capability.

faqs.question: Can mediastinitis be prevented?
faqs.answer: Post-surgical mediastinitis is reduced by giving prophylactic antibiotics within 60 minutes of incision, tight perioperative blood glucose control in diabetic patients, and minimising bone wax use. Treating dental infections before elective cardiac surgery prevents the odontogenic route.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Empirical treatment typically combines vancomycin (to cover MRSA) with a broad-spectrum agent such as piperacillin-tazobactam or a carbapenem for Gram-negative coverage. The regimen is narrowed once culture results are available and continued for 4–6 weeks intravenously.

faqs.question: What does a mediastinitis CT scan show?
faqs.answer: CT of the chest with contrast is the gold-standard imaging. It shows mediastinal widening, air or fluid collections between chest structures, sternal dehiscence, and spread into the pleural or pericardial space. It also maps the extent of neck involvement in descending infections, guiding surgical planning.

faqs.question: What is descending necrotising mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Descending necrotising mediastinitis is a rapidly spreading deep neck infection, usually from a dental abscess or Ludwig angina, that travels down fascial planes into the mediastinum. It is among the most dangerous forms of mediastinitis, with mortality of 25–40% even with aggressive surgery and antibiotics.

faqs.question: Does diabetes increase the risk of mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Patients with diabetes have approximately three times the risk of post-sternotomy mediastinitis compared with non-diabetic patients. High blood sugar impairs white blood cell function and tissue perfusion. Keeping blood glucose well controlled before, during, and after cardiac surgery meaningfully reduces this risk.

faqs.question: What is vacuum-assisted closure (VAC) in mediastinitis?
faqs.answer: Vacuum-assisted closure uses a foam sponge placed in the open sternal wound connected to a suction device that removes fluid, reduces bacterial load, and draws wound edges together. VAC dressings are changed every 2–3 days under sedation until the wound is clean enough for definitive surgical closure, and have improved survival to 70–90% at major centres.

faqs.question: Can mediastinitis come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Recurrence occurs in roughly 5–15% of cases, usually due to residual infected bone, retained foreign material, or an untreated source such as a persistent dental focus. Relapse presents with returning fever and wound problems and requires further surgical exploration.

faqs.question: When should I call my surgeon after sternotomy?
faqs.answer: Call immediately if you notice redness, swelling, or drainage at the incision, feel the sternum clicking or shifting when you move or cough, develop a fever above 38°C, or feel generally much more unwell than expected. These findings need same-day surgical assessment.

faqs.question: Can mediastinitis affect the heart?
faqs.answer: Yes. Infection can spread to the pericardium (causing pericarditis or pericardial effusion), erode bypass graft anastomoses, or trigger endocarditis on heart valves. Cardiac tamponade from infected pericardial fluid is a life-threatening complication requiring emergency drainage.

faqs.question: Is mediastinitis contagious?
faqs.answer: No. Mediastinitis is caused by bacteria from your own body — skin flora, oral bacteria, or gut contents — entering the mediastinum through a surgical wound or perforation. It cannot be transmitted from person to person under normal circumstances.

faqs.question: What is fibrosing mediastinitis and how is it different?
faqs.answer: Fibrosing mediastinitis is a non-infectious condition where excessive scar tissue forms in the mediastinum, usually as a reaction to a previous Histoplasma fungal infection or sarcoidosis. Unlike acute mediastinitis it does not present with fever and wound signs but with gradual breathlessness and obstruction of chest veins or airways over months to years.

faqs.question: What happens if mediastinitis is not treated quickly?
faqs.answer: Untreated mediastinitis carries mortality exceeding 50%. Infection spreads through the chest causing septic shock, erodes major blood vessels, compresses the heart, or extends into the pleural cavities. Even a few hours of delay in surgical debridement significantly worsens outcomes, which is why rapid recognition and emergency surgery are essential.

metaDescription: Mediastinitis is a life-threatening chest infection after heart surgery or esophageal injury. Learn symptoms, emergency signs, surgery and survival rates.
